dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, respond for breathing difficulties at [COMPANY_NAME] Veterans Care Center, room 135.
Hello sir, I'm Casey, an EMT with the rescue squad. What brought you to call 911 tonight?
He's been struggling to breathe since just before we called for help. It started earlier and is still ongoing.
Mr. Smith, can you hear me?
(eyes open slightly, looks towards the voice)
Can you tell me your name?
(nods slowly)
Mr. Smith, we're going to clear your airway. It should help you breathe easier.
Breathing is labored with rapid rate-looks like his respiratory effort is significant.
Respirations are at 35 breaths per minute and deep.
No cyanosis is observed around the lips or nail beds.
Thank you for letting us help. Rapid breathing is working hard to get oxygen.
Pulse oximetry reading is 86 percent, so we'll help improve that.
Pulse is 85 beats per minute, and blood pressure is stable at 139 over 70.
Pulse is regular and strong at 85 beats per minute.
Blood pressure is at 139 over 70.
You're not reporting pain or fever. Is that correct?
I see from your chart that you have heart failure, atrial fibrillation, and a pacemaker.
At the moment, I am unable to obtain a blood sugar reading due to inability to access a sample.
You mentioned no pain or discomfort-CPAP may help with your breathing.
Thanks, we'll keep this in mind throughout our care.
I am hearing rales in both upper and lower lung fields.
Your heart is currently in a regular sinus rhythm.
You are in a Fowler's position, which is good for your breathing-we'll keep you this way.
I will now deploy CPAP to help improve your breathing.
CPAP is now active and helping you breathe. You might feel pressure in your chest.
Given these findings, we are following protocol 2-3 for possible congestive heart failure.
Let me suction to clear your airway and help ease your breathing.
Pulse has increased to 107 beats per minute.
Respiration rate is slightly improved to 25 breaths per minute.
Blood pressure is now 159 over 72.
Oxygen saturation has improved to 90 percent.
Heart rhythm has shifted to sinus tachycardia.
12-lead ECG was just completed for further heart analysis.
Still unable to determine blood sugar, but there are no signs of low sugar levels.
Breathing seems to ease now with CPAP support-no new pain reported.
Let me adjust CPAP slightly for comfort and better breath support.
12-lead ECG revealed sinus tachycardia and a possible bundle branch block.
I will start an IV line in one of your extremity veins for transport readiness.
Mr. Smith, we are transporting you to [COMPANY_NAME] for further evaluation.
You'll now be placed on a stretcher and into the ambulance-please stay still.
Four people will help transfer you carefully-you were breathing labored.
You are now in the back of the ambulance, secured, and breathing via CPAP.
You are being closely monitored during transport for any changes in condition.
Vitals are responding well and staying within expected limits so far.
Breathing remains stable and no alarming signs have developed.
The ambulance is moving now with you safely onboard towards the hospital.
Mr. Smith, you remain alert and oriented during this time.
We follow standard procedures and continue with careful transport.
Transport progress is smooth with CPAP providing continuous breath support.
[COMPANY_NAME]'s medical team is aware and ready to receive you.
No significant changes in vital signs during this early part of transport.
We are on schedule, and everything looks coordinated for a safe arrival.
Necessary details have been prepared for the hospital prior to arrival.
Pulse oximetry remains stable at 90 percent.
IV line is secure and prepared for any use if needed during transport.
Cardiac monitoring remains in use to observe heart activity.
We'll share updates during the rest of your transport as needed.
CPAP still appears to be helping and you are a bit more comfortable.
Four-lead ECG was taken prior to CPAP deployment.
Staff noted he was generally alert and oriented on arrival.
EKG readings remain in sinus tachycardia-nothing to worry about now.
You continue to report no pain-another good sign during transport.
We can't check your blood sugar yet, but we don't detect issues with low sugar.
Your breathing seems to keep improving slightly with CPAP.
IV is staying in place through the remainder of transport-ready if needed.
CPAP is still doing well to support your breathing and comfort.
[COMPANY_NAME] has been fully updated with all our findings and your condition.
Hospital is expecting you within ten minutes.
Mr. Smith, we are taking you to the trauma bay for prompt care.
No new symptoms have developed during movement or monitoring.
Airway was suctioned again to help with breathing, and he remains stable.
Slight CPAP adjustments may occur for a more comfortable pressurized breath.
No further deterioration observed so far during transport.
IV remains in place and is ready for potential use in the hospital.
Your skin remains pink on both extremities with good perfusion signs.
Breathing remains well supported by CPAP throughout the ambulance ride.
Vitals are watching well, and we'll continue monitoring until the hospital.
CPAP support remains effective and steady during transport.
All preparations for handoff are now complete.
Hospital staff has received full documentation and ECG findings.
You've remained alert on the trip with no signs of new symptoms.
We are nearing the hospital with fifteen minutes to final arrival.
You will now be handed off to the trauma team who is fully ready for you.
Trauma bay seven is in sight, and we will take you to them now.
We are on schedule for arrival at [COMPANY_NAME].
IV remains safe and CPAP is helping during final hospital approach.
